translation: Kaf, Ha, Ya, 'Ayn, Sad
commentary:
**كٓهيعٓصٓ**

This Surah begins with the mysterious letters Kaf Ha Ya 'Ayn Sad. As with other such openings in the Quran, these disjointed letters remind us that the Quran is composed of the very letters familiar to the Arabs, yet they could never produce anything like it. These letters set the stage for what follows—a profound story of divine mercy and miraculous power.

translation: [This is] a mention of the mercy of your Lord to His servant Zechariah
commentary:
**ذِكْرُ رَحْمَتِ رَبِّكَ عَبْدَهُۥ زَكَرِيَّآ**

This is the mention of the mercy of your Master to His slave Zakariya. The word "mention" (dhikr) connects this narrative to the broader context of Surah Al-Kahf that came before it. Allah ﷻ is building a case, addressing Christian misconceptions not by attacking their beliefs about 'Isa (AS) directly, but by establishing a foundation through the story of Zakariya (AS).

The structure of this ayah is remarkable. Allah ﷻ says "to His slave"—abdahu—and then clarifies who that slave is: Zakariya. This grammatical construction emphasizes the honor of being Allah's slave. Before we even know who Zakariya (AS) is or what his story entails, we're drawn directly into the narrative itself, without lengthy introductions or background information.

translation: When he called to his Lord a private supplication
commentary:
**إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥ نِدَآءً خَفِيًّا**

When he called upon his Master with a hidden call. The word نِدَآء (nida) typically refers to a loud call, often used when calling from a distance or in desperation. Yet immediately after this word, Allah ﷻ adds خَفِيًّا (khafiyya)—secret, hidden, quiet. This creates a striking contrast: he called out desperately, yet kept his voice low.

The word khafiyya doesn't just mean quiet—it means so hidden that people don't even know it exists, a secret so well-guarded that no one is even looking for it. Zakariya (AS) was desperate in his need, but out of respect and humility before Allah ﷻ, he kept his voice low. This teaches us profound adab (etiquette) in supplication: even in our most desperate moments, we maintain dignified composure before our Creator.

translation: He said, "My Lord, indeed my bones have weakened, and my head has filled with white, and never have I been in my supplication to You, my Lord, unhappy
commentary:
**قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّى وَهَنَ ٱلْعَظْمُ مِنِّى وَٱشْتَعَلَ ٱلرَّأْسُ شَيْبًا وَلَمْ أَكُنۢ بِدُعَآئِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا**

He said, "My Master, indeed my bones have become weak within me, and my head has burst into flames with gray hair, and never have I been in my supplication to You, my Master, unhappy."

The imagery in this dua is extraordinary. Zakariya (AS) says وَهَنَ ٱلْعَظْمُ مِنِّى—"the bone has become weak from me." Notice he uses the singular "bone" as if all his bones feel like one, and that single bone has become weak. The entire body is like a building, and bones are its pillars. When the pillars weaken, everything weakens.

The word وَهَنَ (wahana) describes something hard becoming soft—like metal rusting, wood becoming soggy, or bones becoming brittle. He says "from me" (minni) as if his bones are no longer truly his, as if there's some foreign creature inside his body. This is arthritis expressed in the most eloquent Arabic possible.

Then comes even more striking imagery: وَٱشْتَعَلَ ٱلرَّأْسُ شَيْبًا—"my head has burst into flames with whiteness." The verb اشْتَعَلَ (ishta'ala) means to catch fire, burn intensely, and be reduced to ash—like a match that flares up and quickly burns out. He compares his white hair to fire because when fire burns something and reduces it to white ash, you cannot restore it. It's beyond repair, at the point of no return.

The word شَيْب (shayb) literally means whiteness or old age. In Arabic poetry, mountain peaks covered in white snow are called shayb, and this is compared to an old man whose peak—his head—has turned white. Zakariya (AS) is saying: I've reached an age I cannot come back from. The deterioration is irreversible.

Yet despite all this, he says: وَلَمْ أَكُنۢ بِدُعَآئِكَ رَبِّ شَقِيًّا—"and never have I been unhappy in calling upon You, my Master." The word شَقِيًّا (shaqiyya) means wretched, unfortunate, or unlucky. He's saying: I've never felt misfortunate when making dua to You. I've never called upon You and felt You didn't listen. Throughout my entire life—until my hair turned gray—I've been optimistic about Your response. I've never been disappointed in You.

This is the foundation of his request: a lifetime of trust in Allah's mercy.

translation: And indeed, I fear the successors after me, and my wife has been barren, so give me from Yourself an heir
commentary:
**وَإِنِّى خِفْتُ ٱلْمَوَٰلِىَ مِن وَرَآءِى وَكَانَتِ ٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرًا فَهَبْ لِى مِن لَّدُنكَ وَلِيًّا**

And indeed, I fear the successors after me, and my wife has been barren, so grant me from Yourself an heir.

The word ٱلْمَوَٰلِىَ (al-mawali) refers to guardians, those who inherit not just wealth but responsibility and legacy. Zakariya (AS) is concerned about who will lead the community after him, who will carry the message forward. He doesn't trust those currently in line to inherit this sacred responsibility. He fears they're not qualified, not the right people to guide the believers.

Then he explains his dilemma: وَكَانَتِ ٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرًا—"my wife has been barren." The word عَاقِر ('aqir) comes from a root meaning to cut or bite. It's used for cutting something at its roots, severing it completely. It's also used for the last child—the one after whom the lineage is cut off. Most significantly, 'aqir refers to a woman who suffered some trauma or sickness that made her unable to bear children.

The verb كَانَتِ (kanat) indicates this has been her condition for a long time—perhaps since youth, something happened that made her this way. So he's saying: If only I could raise someone myself, I would. But I'm too old, and my wife has long been unable to conceive.

فَهَبْ لِى مِن لَّدُنكَ وَلِيًّا—"So grant me from Your own special presence a guardian." He's not just asking for any child through natural means. He's saying: I don't know what to do. The normal avenues are closed. So grant me someone from Your special power, from beyond the natural order.

translation: Who will inherit me and inherit from the family of Jacob. And make him, my Lord, pleasing [to You]
commentary:
**يَرِثُنِى وَيَرِثُ مِنْ ءَالِ يَعْقُوبَ ۖ وَٱجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا**

Who will inherit me and inherit from the family of Ya'qub. And make him, my Master, pleasing.

The inheritance Zakariya (AS) speaks of is not wealth—prophets don't leave wealth as inheritance. This is the inheritance of knowledge, of prophetic responsibility, of leadership over the community. 

Notice the precision: يَرِثُ مِنْ ءَالِ يَعْقُوبَ—"inherit from the family of Ya'qub." He uses the particle مِنْ (min—"from") very carefully. He doesn't say "inherit the family of Ya'qub" as if everything about them is worth inheriting. He says "from the family"—meaning there are members of Ya'qub's family not worth taking from, but there are some qualities and teachings that should definitely be inherited.

وَٱجْعَلْهُ رَبِّ رَضِيًّا—"And make him, my Master, pleasing." The word رَضِيًّا (radiyya) means content, someone who doesn't want more, someone satisfied with what they have. This is essential for religious leadership. When you enter public service—and religious leadership is the ultimate public service—you shouldn't be in it for yourself. You must be content with whatever you have.

When someone is truly content, they're able to communicate that contentment to others. Their tranquility impacts their students, their congregation. But when they themselves lack contentment, they cannot convey it to others. This is why Zakariya (AS) asks that his heir be someone who is radiyya—content—and also someone with whom Allah ﷻ is pleased (mardiyya).

translation: [He was told], "O Zechariah, indeed We give you good tidings of a boy whose name will be John. We have not assigned to any before [this] name
commentary:
**يَـٰزَكَرِيَّآ إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ بِغُلَـٰمٍ ٱسْمُهُۥ يَحْيَىٰ لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا**

"O Zakariya, indeed We give you good news of a boy whose name is Yahya. We have not assigned to anyone before him this name."

The response comes directly: O Zakariya. Allah ﷻ addresses him by name, a tremendous honor. إِنَّا نُبَشِّرُكَ—"We give you good news." This isn't just information; it's good news, glad tidings.

بِغُلَـٰمٍ ٱسْمُهُۥ يَحْيَىٰ—"of a boy whose name is Yahya." Even before he's born, Allah ﷻ names him. The name يَحْيَىٰ (Yahya) means "he lives." After Zakariya (AS) just described himself as old, deteriorated, and at death's door, Allah ﷻ says: your son's name is "He Lives." It's a direct response to the desperation he expressed.

The name also carries deeper significance. Yahya (AS) would later be known as someone who brought dead hearts to life, who revived faith in people's souls. From this perspective, Yahya truly means "the one who gives life."

لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا—"We have not assigned to anyone before him this name." The word سَمِيًّا (samiyya) can mean someone with the same name, but it also means someone with the same qualities or someone born in the same miraculous way. No one before has been given these particular qualities or been born under these specific circumstances.

translation: He said, "My Lord, how will I have a boy when my wife has been barren and I have reached extreme old age
commentary:
**قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَكَانَتِ ٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرًا وَقَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ ٱلْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا**

He said, "My Master, how can I have a boy when my wife has been barren and I have reached extreme old age?"

Zakariya (AS) repeats himself, though he's already been given the answer. He's not doubting Allah's power—he's expressing wonder and amazement. This is human nature when faced with the miraculous.

He restates: وَكَانَتِ ٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرًا—"my wife has been barren." Then adds a new description of his old age: وَقَدْ بَلَغْتُ مِنَ ٱلْكِبَرِ عِتِيًّا—"and I have reached from old age, 'itiyya."

The word عِتِيًّا ('itiyya) comes from a root meaning extreme rebellion and arrogance, someone who has reached such a peak of defiance that no one can stand in their face. It's incredibly poetic: he describes his old age as a bully, a rebel that has overtaken him and that he cannot fight off. First, he described flames on his head turning it white beyond redemption. Now he says his old age is this overwhelming force, this tyrant he cannot resist. The language powerfully conveys his sense of impossibility.

translation: [An angel] said, "Thus [it will be]; your Lord says, 'It is easy for Me, for I created you before, while you were nothing
commentary:
**قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ قَالَ رَبُّكَ هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْـًٔا**

He said, "Thus it will be. Your Master says: It is easy for Me, and I created you before when you were nothing."

The response is definitive: كَذَٰلِكَ—"Thus it will be." My word is My word. This is happening.

قَالَ رَبُّكَ—"Your Master says"—note that while angels are conveying this message, Zakariya (AS) addresses Allah ﷻ directly, and Allah ﷻ is described as speaking directly. This is crucial: you don't need intermediaries to speak to Allah ﷻ. This ayah itself refutes the Catholic notion that you must go through saints and angels to reach God. Zakariya (AS) knew that even though angels were speaking to him, he could and should speak directly to Allah ﷻ.

هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ—"It is easy for Me." The word هَيِّنٌ (hayyin) means completely effortless. Something can be easy but still require some effort. Hayyin means you don't even feel like you're working—it's that effortless.

وَقَدْ خَلَقْتُكَ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ تَكُ شَيْـًٔا—"And I created you before when you were nothing." Notice it doesn't even have the letter ن (nun) on شَيْـًٔا—you were beyond nothing, absolutely nothing at all. If I created you from nothing, creating a child from your old age is nothing in comparison.

translation: [Zechariah] said, "My Lord, make for me a sign." He said, "Your sign is that you will not speak to the people for three nights, [being] sound
commentary:
**قَالَ رَبِّ ٱجْعَل لِّىٓ ءَايَةً ۚ قَالَ ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَ لَيَالٍ سَوِيًّا**

He said, "My Master, appoint for me a sign." He said, "Your sign is that you will not speak to the people for three nights, being sound."

Zakariya (AS) asks for an ayah—a sign. Not because he doubts, but because he wants confirmation, something tangible to mark this miraculous promise.

The response: ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَ لَيَالٍ سَوِيًّا—"Your sign is that you will not speak to the people for three nights, being sound."

Notice the texture of this Surah—everything is characterized by darkness and night. Old age, flames that turn to gray ash, the quiet secret prayer, and now three nights. The entire opening creates this dark, somber, nighttime atmosphere.

ثَلَـٰثَ لَيَالٍ—"three nights"—could mean three consecutive nights or three complete nights.

سَوِيًّا—"being sound"—is particularly important. The word سَوِيًّا means complete, whole, healthy. You won't be able to speak to people, even though you're perfectly healthy. You'll have full use of your tongue, you'll be completely well, but Allah ﷻ will miraculously remove your ability to speak. This isn't illness or injury—it's a supernatural sign.

translation: So he came out to his people from the prayer chamber and signaled to them to exalt [Allah] in the morning and afternoon
commentary:
**فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ مِنَ ٱلْمِحْرَابِ فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِمْ أَن سَبِّحُوا۟ بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا**

So he came out to his people from the prayer chamber and signaled to them to exalt Allah in the morning and afternoon.

The word ٱلْمِحْرَابِ (al-mihrab) deserves attention. Its root means war or battle. The mihrab is the place where you wage war—but not against external enemies. It's where you battle your own nafs, your lower desires. It's where you struggle in worship, where you engage in the real jihad of the soul. This is why the Quran mentions that the jinn made for Sulayman (AS) مَحَارِيب (maharib)—places of worship, but also arsenals in the sense of spiritual fortifications.

The true weapon of the believer is dua, and it's wielded in the mihrab. This is the real arsenal, the place where spiritual battles are fought and won.

فَخَرَجَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ—"So he came out to his people." They're waiting for him. He's their imam, their leader. But he cannot speak.

فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَيْهِمْ—"and he signaled to them." The word أَوْحَىٰٓ (awha) here means to gesture or indicate through signs. He could only use gestures and sign language.

أَن سَبِّحُوا۟ بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا—"to exalt Allah in the morning and afternoon." Through his gestures, he communicated: just make tasbih, glorify Allah, morning and evening. The people understood and they listened.

This concludes the opening passage of Surah Maryam, which establishes the foundation for what follows: if Yahya (AS) could be born to an elderly, incapable father and a barren mother, then the birth of 'Isa (AS) to Maryam—though miraculous—should not lead anyone to conclude divinity. Allah ﷻ's power transcends all natural limitations, and miracles are signs of His power, not proof of divinity in His creation.

translation: [Allah] said, "O John, take the Scripture with determination." And We gave him judgement [while yet] a boy
commentary:
يَـٰيَحْيَىٰ خُذِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِقُوَّةٍ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْحُكْمَ صَبِيًّا

The narrative shifts immediately from the birth of Yahya (AS) to his maturity and readiness for prophethood. Allah ﷻ addresses him directly: "O Yahya, hold on to the book with all your might." This command isn't something said once, but represents continuous divine instruction throughout Yahya's (AS) formative years. The emphasis on holding the book firmly relates to his future role as the right hand and supporter of Isa (AS). To be a qualified assistant to a prophet, Yahya (AS) needed profound knowledge of the Tawrah.

The second part of the ayah presents a striking contrast: "And We had given him wisdom while still a child." The word حُكْم signifies maturity, firmness and sound judgment. We see this same concept when Yusuf (AS) grows up in the castle of Egypt, and again with Musa (AS) in Surah Al-Qasas. The word صَبِيًّا normally refers to a child in age, but it's also used for adults who act childishly, those who lack maturity. Allah ﷻ is highlighting something extraordinary: even though Yahya (AS) was expected to lack maturity due to his young age, Allah ﷻ granted him wisdom anyway. These are two qualities that don't normally go together, yet they converged in Yahya (AS).

translation: And affection from Us and purity, and he was fearing of Allah
commentary:
وَحَنَانًا مِّن لَّدُنَّا وَزَكَوٰةً ۖ وَكَانَ تَقِيًّا

The word حَنَانًا (hananan) describes a unique quality in Yahya (AS). In Arabic, when a mother misses her child, she experiences hanan—a persistent longing that draws her back repeatedly. She might be working in the kitchen, but keeps returning to check on her sleeping baby, unable to stay away for long. This same quality manifested in Yahya's (AS) relationship with Allah ﷻ. He couldn't go too long without engaging in dhikr. He felt an emptiness that drove him to return again and again to remembrance of Allah ﷻ. This persistent spiritual longing could only come directly from Allah ﷻ.

The ayah continues: "and purity, and he was consciously mindful of Allah." The word زَكَوٰة here refers to purity of character and chastity. According to some scholars, Yahya (AS) never married, and maintained such spiritual focus that he never even looked at women with desire. His entire existence was characterized by an unusual spirituality. The word تَقِيًّا emphasizes how watchful and protective he was of his character, constantly guarding himself against anything that might compromise his relationship with Allah ﷻ.

translation: And dutiful to his parents, and he was not a disobedient tyrant
commentary:
وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ وَلَمْ يَكُن جَبَّارًا عَصِيًّا

"And he was good to both his parents, and he wasn't a tyrant, nor was he disobedient." This description isn't coincidental—it echoes concerns from the previous surah about a child who might grow up to be oppressive and force his parents into disbelief. Allah ﷻ specifically highlights Yahya's (AS) goodness to his parents because this is a prophetic tradition, a special characteristic of those chosen by Allah ﷻ.

While being good to parents is expected of any righteous person, Allah ﷻ is emphasizing something profound here: having the opportunity to be good to parents is itself a gift from Allah ﷻ. It's not just an obligation, but a privilege that reflects divine favor.

translation: And peace be upon him the day he was born and the day he dies and the day he is raised alive
commentary:
وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْهِ يَوْمَ وُلِدَ وَيَوْمَ يَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ يُبْعَثُ حَيًّا

"And peace be upon him the day he was born, and the day he dies, and the day he will be resurrected alive." The word سَلَـٰمٌ carries multiple meanings. It indicates that Yahya (AS) was born safely, despite his mother being beyond the typical age of childbearing. But salam also means honor—he was honored from the moment of his birth. This divine peace and honor extends to three critical moments: his birth, his death as a martyr, and his resurrection on the Day of Judgment.

translation: And mention, [O Muhammad], in the Book [the story of] Mary, when she withdrew from her family to a place toward the east
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَرْيَمَ إِذِ ٱنتَبَذَتْ مِنْ أَهْلِهَا مَكَانًا شَرْقِيًّا

The narrative now transitions to Maryam (AS): "Mention in the Book Maryam, when she withdrew from her family to a place toward the east." The word ٱنتَبَذَتْ is significant. We encounter a form of this word in Surah Al-Baqarah: "they threw it away," treating something as worthless. When someone practices intibadh upon themselves, they view themselves as worthless and withdraw from others' company, thinking, "I'm an embarrassment to my family. I don't belong here. I need to be away."

This isn't about superiority or wanting solitude for personal comfort. Maryam (AS) withdrew because of profound humility. She was so inclined toward worshipping Allah ﷻ, so devoted to His remembrance, that she couldn't even spend extended time with the people she loved most—her own family. Her mother had dedicated her to Allah's ﷻ service before birth, and as a result, Maryam (AS) developed an extraordinary love for dhikr. She excused herself from family life not out of disdain, but out of overwhelming devotion.

The phrase مَكَانًا شَرْقِيًّا, "a place toward the east," refers to the eastern side of Masjid Al-Aqsa. Some scholars note that she chose this location to be away from the regular population of men who came to pray. While her family quarters were closer to the main area, she deliberately chose the eastern wing to maintain complete separation and privacy for worship.

translation: And she took, in seclusion from them, a screen. Then We sent to her Our Angel, and he represented himself to her as a well-proportioned man
commentary:
فَٱتَّخَذَتْ مِن دُونِهِمْ حِجَابًا فَأَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهَا رُوحَنَا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَهَا بَشَرًا سَوِيًّا

"And she took a barrier apart from them. Then We sent to her Our spirit, and he appeared to her as a well-proportioned man." There is consensus among Muslim scholars that رُوحَنَ, "Our spirit," refers to Jibreel (AS). He came to her in human form—not as an angel, but appearing as a mortal man. The word سَوِيًّا means upright and well-formed, but also suggests good character. He appeared as a righteous, healthy man standing before her.

Imagine the scene: Maryam (AS) is alone, behind a curtain, engaged in worship, and suddenly a man pulls back the curtain and appears. Regardless of how righteous he might look, the situation is alarming for a woman in complete privacy.

translation: She said, "Indeed, I seek refuge in the Most Merciful from you, [so leave me], if you should be fearing of Allah
commentary:
قَالَتْ إِنِّىٓ أَعُوذُ بِٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مِنكَ إِن كُنتَ تَقِيًّا

Her immediate response reflects her character: "She said, 'Indeed, I seek refuge in the Most Merciful from you, if you are God-conscious.'" Notice the repeated use of الرَّحْمَـٰنِ throughout this surah. This is particularly meaningful for a Christian audience, as they frequently refer to God as "Most Merciful." Allah ﷻ is appealing to their understanding.

The phrase إِن كُنتَ تَقِيًّا can be interpreted in two ways. First: "If you have any consciousness of Allah ﷻ at all, then you won't do what I think you're about to do." She's warning him that if he has any fear of Allah ﷻ, he should stop. Some scholars offer a second interpretation: "Even if you are righteous, I still seek refuge from you." Because righteousness is no guarantee of safety in such a vulnerable situation.

translation: He said, "I am only the messenger of your Lord to give you [news of] a pure boy
commentary:
قَالَ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلَـٰمًا زَكِيًّا

Jibreel (AS) reassures her: "He said, 'I am only the messenger of your Lord to give you news of a pure boy.'" He clarifies immediately: "I didn't come because I want to. I'm here because I have to be. I'm on a mission." His role is specific—to announce that he will grant her a child. The word زَكِيًّا describes this child as both intelligent and pure in character. But how will Jibreel (AS) "grant" her a child? Through the divine breath, the ruh that he will blow into her.

translation: She said, "How can I have a boy while no man has touched me and I have not been unchaste
commentary:
قَالَتْ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌ وَلَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا

Maryam's (AS) response is natural and confused: "She said, 'How can I have a boy while no man has touched me and I have not been unchaste?'" She presents two facts: first, no man has ever touched her—she's never been married. Second, وَلَمْ أَكُ بَغِيًّا—" and I've never been rebellious." This means she never went through any phase of disobedience, never had a time when she strayed from righteousness. How then could this possibly happen?

translation: He said, "Thus [it will be]; your Lord says, 'It is easy for Me, and We will make him a sign to the people and a mercy from Us. And it is a matter [already] decreed
commentary:
قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ ۖ وَلِنَجْعَلَهُۥٓ ءَايَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَرَحْمَةً مِّنَّا ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرًا مَّقْضِيًّا

Jibreel (AS) responds: "He said, 'Thus said your Lord: It is easy for Me.'" Listen to these words carefully: هُوَ عَلَىَّ هَيِّنٌ —"It is completely easy for Him, without any difficulty." Haven't we heard these exact words before in this very surah? Yes, when Zakariyya (AS) questioned how he could have a child in his old age. History repeats itself. The same patterns emerge. The same divine words are uttered again, generations apart, demonstrating that Allah's ﷻ power transcends all natural limitations.

"And so that We may make him a sign for the people and a mercy from Us." Isa (AS) is a manifestation of Allah's ﷻ word. Christians say, "Jesus is the Word," and we don't disagree—the Quran affirms this. Isa (AS) is indeed a word from Allah ﷻ, a miraculous sign for humanity. وَكَانَ أَمْرًا مَّقْضِيًّا—"And it is a matter already decreed." It's done. The decision has been declared and sealed.

translation: So she conceived him, and she withdrew with him to a remote place
commentary:
۞ فَحَمَلَتْهُ فَٱنتَبَذَتْ بِهِۦ مَكَانًا قَصِيًّا

"So she conceived him and withdrew with him to a remote place." She became pregnant, and as it became increasingly obvious that she was carrying a child, she withdrew even further. She went as far away as she possibly could, to the most distant place, wanting to be completely removed from anyone who might see her condition and question her.

translation: And the pains of childbirth drove her to the trunk of a palm tree. She said, "Oh, I wish I had died before this and was in oblivion, forgotten
commentary:
فَأَجَآءَهَا ٱلْمَخَاضُ إِلَىٰ جِذْعِ ٱلنَّخْلَةِ قَالَتْ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى مِتُّ قَبْلَ هَـٰذَا وَكُنتُ نَسْيًا مَّنسِيًّا

"And the pains of childbirth drove her to the trunk of a palm tree. She said, 'Oh, I wish I had died before this and was completely forgotten.'" When the labor pains came, she found herself at the base of a palm tree, unable to go any further. In this moment of overwhelming difficulty, she cries out: يَـٰلَيْتَنِى مِتُّ قَبْلَ هَـٰذَا—"If only I had died before this happened."

Scholars have discussed whether it's permissible to wish for death, as the Prophet ﷺ taught us not to wish for death. However, the overwhelming understanding is that Maryam (AS) isn't wishing for death out of despair with Allah ﷻ, but out of the overwhelming burden of responsibility and anticipated humiliation. It's similar to how a disbeliever on Judgment Day will wish he had been a blade of grass, something that wouldn't be questioned. The sentiment isn't "I want to die," but rather "I don't know if I can bear this responsibility. I don't know how people will humiliate me and what they'll say about me."

نَسْيًا مَّنسِيًّا—"completely and utterly forgotten"—is an adjective upon an adjective for emphasis. She wishes she was someone nobody even remembered, someone whose name was never mentioned at all.

translation: But he called her from below her, "Do not grieve; your Lord has provided beneath you a stream
commentary:
فَنَادَىٰهَا مِن تَحْتِهَآ أَلَّا تَحْزَنِى قَدْ جَعَلَ رَبُّكِ تَحْتَكِ سَرِيًّا

"Then he called her from below her, 'Do not grieve; your Lord has provided beneath you a stream.'" There's a difference of opinion about who is calling here. Some scholars say this is Isa (AS) as he was born, speaking immediately. Others say Jibreel (AS) returned to the palm tree where she was. However, he didn't approach her from the front, which would have been indecent given her vulnerable state. Instead, he remained at the bottom of the hill while she was higher up, and called to her from that distance to maintain her privacy.

The message is powerful: "Don't grieve." What is she grieving about? The humiliation she anticipates when people accuse her. But the voice reassures her: "Your Lord has placed right beneath you a small stream of water." Allah ﷻ has provided for her immediate needs.

translation: And shake toward you the trunk of the palm tree; it will drop upon you ripe, fresh dates
commentary:
وَهُزِّىٓ إِلَيْكِ بِجِذْعِ ٱلنَّخْلَةِ تُسَـٰقِطْ عَلَيْكِ رُطَبًا جَنِيًّا

"And shake toward you the trunk of the palm tree; it will drop upon you ripe, fresh dates." Now consider this instruction: shake the trunk of a palm tree. Is a tree trunk easy or difficult to shake, especially for a woman who has just given birth and is in extreme pain? Yet women during delivery experience extraordinary bursts of energy—this is well documented. Allah ﷻ gave Maryam (AS) this surge of strength to grab the trunk and shake it. When she did, the dates began falling, and the branches lowered themselves toward her. Her food and drink were miraculously provided.

translation: So eat and drink and be contented. And if you see from among humanity anyone, say, 'Indeed, I have vowed to the Most Merciful abstention, so I will not speak today to [any] man
commentary:
فَكُلِى وَٱشْرَبِى وَقَرِّى عَيْنًا ۖ فَإِمَّا تَرَيِنَّ مِنَ ٱلْبَشَرِ أَحَدًا فَقُولِىٓ إِنِّى نَذَرْتُ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ صَوْمًا فَلَنْ أُكَلِّمَ ٱلْيَوْمَ إِنسِيًّا

"So eat and drink and be contented. And if you see from among humanity anyone, say, 'Indeed, I have vowed to the Most Merciful a fast, so I will not speak today to any person.'"

The phrase قَرِّى عَيْنًا literally means "cool the eye," which refers to stopping crying and finding contentment. Anyone who has witnessed childbirth knows that mothers often cry after delivery—not just tears of joy, but also tears of emotional release. Allah ﷻ is telling her: "Stop crying. Relax. Calm down. It's okay."

Then comes a crucial instruction: "If you happen to see any man at all, then say: 'I have devoted myself to the Most Merciful with a fast, so today I will not speak to any human being.'" This was a form of fasting that existed before Islam—a fast from speaking. It was a recognized spiritual practice where someone would declare a certain number of days during which they would speak to no one.

This raises an interesting question: how can she say "I'm fasting from speech" without speaking? Doesn't saying those words break the fast? The answer lies in understanding that she would communicate this message once, and then maintain her silence. Additionally, the previous surah has already given us insight into communication without direct speech—through writing and gestures. The key is that she can indicate, she can gesture, she can find ways to communicate the concept without engaging in conversation.

translation: Then she brought him to her people, carrying him. They said, "O Mary, you have certainly done a thing unprecedented
commentary:
فَأَتَتْ بِهِۦ قَوْمَهَا تَحْمِلُهُۥ ۖ قَالُوا۟ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ لَقَدْ جِئْتِ شَيْـًٔا فَرِيًّا

Eventually—some say after forty days—Allah ﷻ gave Maryam (AS) the courage to return. "Then she brought him to her people, carrying him. They said, 'O Maryam, you have certainly done a terrible thing.'"

The word فَرِيًّا describes something both unexpected and enormously serious—a major transgression. They're essentially saying: "You have committed a horrible offense that no one saw coming from you."

translation: O sister of Aaron, your father was not a man of evil, nor was your mother unchaste
commentary:
يَـٰٓأُخْتَ هَـٰرُونَ مَا كَانَ أَبُوكِ ٱمْرَأَ سَوْءٍ وَمَا كَانَتْ أُمُّكِ بَغِيًّا

"O sister of Harun, your father was not a man of evil, nor was your mother unchaste." The phrase "sister of Harun" has sparked questions. Some people ask: "Is this saying Maryam (AS) is the sister of Harun (AS), the brother of Musa (AS)? Does that mean Maryam (AS) is the one who walked by the river when Musa (AS) was placed in the basket? Is Isa (AS) the nephew of Musa (AS)?"

This confusion actually arose during the Prophet's ﷺ time. When the Companions went to make da'wah to certain Christians and recited this ayah, the Christians questioned this phrasing. The Companion didn't know how to respond, so he returned to the Prophet ﷺ and asked. The Prophet ﷺ explained: "Don't you know that people, out of love for the prophets, name their children after them?"

There are two main interpretations. First, Maryam (AS) may have actually had a sibling named Harun—it was a common name in their community. Second, just as people from Bani Tamim were called "brother of Tamim," people from a particular lineage would be addressed by reference to a notable ancestor. Since Harun (AS) was a beloved figure in the history of Bani Israel, they might address someone from his lineage as "sister of Harun" or "brother of Harun" as a way of saying "you're from his people, from that noble lineage."

The message is clear: "How could you do this? Your father was not a man of evil, and your mother was not rebellious either. You come from a righteous family—how could you bring this shame?"

translation: So she pointed to him. They said, "How can we speak to one who is in the cradle a child
commentary:
فَأَشَارَتْ إِلَيْهِ ۖ قَالُوا۟ كَيْفَ نُكَلِّمُ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ صَبِيًّا

Maryam (AS) says nothing. She cannot say anything—she's fasting from speech. "So she pointed to him. They said, 'How can we speak to one who is in the cradle, a child?'" 

She simply points to the baby. The people are baffled: "How can someone speak who's just been placed in the cradle? He's not old enough—how is he going to speak?"

And it is here that the miracle unfolds, as the next passage will reveal the infant Isa (AS) speaking in defense of his mother's honor.

translation: [Jesus] said, "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet
commentary:
قَالَ إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّا

One of the most remarkable moments in the Quran is the speech of Prophet Isa (AS) as an infant. Imagine the scene: a baby, cradled in his mother's arms, speaking with the authority and wisdom of a mighty messenger of Allah ﷻ. This was not the weak voice of a newborn, but a powerful declaration before a crowd.

He begins with the most fundamental truth: إِنِّى عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ- "I am indeed the slave of Allah." This is the first thing he establishes about his identity. Not divinity, not sonship, but servitude to Allah ﷻ. This is the Islamic position on Isa (AS) - complete and total submission to the One God.

Then he declares: ءَاتَىٰنِىَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ- "He has given me the Book." Notice the past tense - the baby has already been given the book. Some might argue this refers to a future promise, but the immediate meaning is more powerful: go ahead and test me. Ask me any question about the scripture. His knowledge of the book is flawless because it came directly from Allah ﷻ, not through human learning. This is why later in his life, Isa (AS) would challenge the rabbis of Bani Israel about their manipulations of the Torah - he knew it better than they did.

وَجَعَلَنِى نَبِيًّا - "And He has made me a prophet." From infancy, his prophethood is established.

translation: And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and zakah as long as I remain alive
commentary:
وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ وَأَوْصَـٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا

The statement وَجَعَلَنِى مُبَارَكًا أَيْنَ مَا كُنتُ - "He has made me blessed wherever I may be" - contains a subtle prophecy. Why mention "wherever I may be"? Isa (AS) did travel - he ascended to the heavens and will return. This phrase encompasses his entire journey, including his miraculous ascension and his future return.

Then comes وَأَوْصَـٰنِى بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ - "And He has enjoined upon me prayer and charity." The word أَوْصَـٰنِى is significant. It's different from simply commanding. Wasiyya in Arabic is advice given with deep concern, like a parent's counsel to a beloved child. It's not just an obligation - it's something recommended with love and care for the person's benefit. Allah ﷻ didn't just command Isa (AS) to establish prayer and give charity; He counseled him with concern and love.

The phrase مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا - "as long as I remain alive" - is another subtle indication. When Isa (AS) returns, he will still be committed to salah and zakah. His obligations continue.

translation: And [made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a wretched tyrant
commentary:
وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَتِى وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِى جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا

This ayah addresses a sensitive reality. While Prophet Yahya (AS) was described as being good to both his parents, Isa (AS) says وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَتِى - "and dutiful to my mother." He mentions only his mother because he has no father to mention. Even as an infant, he acknowledges this truth without shame, because there is no shame in the decree of Allah ﷻ.

This declaration is also important because in some Christian literature, there are accounts of Isa (AS) speaking harshly to his mother. The Quran exonerates him from this accusation. He was commanded to be good to his mother, and he fulfilled this command.

وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِى جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا - "And He has not made me a tyrant, wretched." The word جَبَّارًا refers to someone who is tyrannical, particularly toward their parents. The word شَقِيًّا means someone deeply unfortunate and miserable. These two qualities are connected - if you are tyrannical to your parents, you become the most unfortunate person. You will have a terrible life filled with misfortune. Isa (AS) is declaring that he is neither of these things.

translation: And peace is on me the day I was born and the day I will die and the day I am raised alive
commentary:
وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىَّ يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا

These words echo what was said about Yahya (AS), but with a beautiful difference. About Yahya (AS), Allah ﷻ said: وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْهِ - "And peace be upon him." Here, Isa (AS) himself says: وَٱلسَّلَـٰمُ عَلَىَّ (was-salamu 'alayya) - "And peace be upon me."

He mentions three critical moments: يَوْمَ وُلِدتُّ - "the day I was born," وَيَوْمَ أَمُوتُ - "the day I will die," and وَيَوْمَ أُبْعَثُ حَيًّا - "and the day I will be raised alive." These are the three most vulnerable moments of human existence, and Allah's peace and protection are upon him in all three.

translation: That is Jesus, the son of Mary - the word of truth about which they are in dispute
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۚ قَوْلَ ٱلْحَقِّ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ

Notice how the rhythm of Surah Maryam suddenly shifts here. From the beginning of Maryam's story through Isa's speech, the ayahs flow with similar rhythmic patterns. Then suddenly, the style changes dramatically: ذَٰلِكَ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ. The long syllables take over. The Quran's rhyme scheme itself serves as subject matter organization - when the content is similar, the rhythm is similar. When the subject requires special attention, the rhythm changes to alert the listener: pay attention, something important is happening here.

Allah ﷻ declares: "That is Isa, the son of Maryam." Then comes قَوْلَ ٱلْحَقِّ - "a statement of truth" or "the word of truth." The Jews said he was illegitimate. The Christians said he was the son of God. One doesn't give him enough respect, the other gives him far too much. This ayah presents the middle path, the truth.

ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ يَمْتَرُونَ  - "about which they dispute." They have doubts where there should be no confusion. The truth is clear, yet they continue to argue.

translation: It is not [befitting] for Allah to take a son; exalted is He! When He decrees an affair, He only says to it, "Be," and it is
commentary:
مَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ أَن يَتَّخِذَ مِن وَلَدٍ ۖ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

Here Allah ﷻ directly addresses the false claim of divine sonship. مَا كَانَ لِلَّهِ أَن يَتَّخِذَ مِن وَلَدٍ - "It is not befitting for Allah to take a son." The phrase "it is not befitting" indicates that this is beneath the majesty and perfection of Allah ﷻ. It's not merely that He chose not to - it's that the very concept contradicts His nature.

سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥٓ - "Glory be to Him." He is far above such allegations.

Then comes the explanation: إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ - "When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is." Why would Allah ﷻ need a son when His creative power is absolute? He speaks and creation comes into existence. The miraculous birth of Isa (AS) is no different from any other act of divine creation.

translation: [Jesus said], "And indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is a straight path
commentary:
وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۚ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ

Some scholars note that this ayah represents a transition where Allah ﷻ instructs Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to declare: وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ - "Indeed, Allah is my Master and your Master." This could also be understood as a continuation of Isa's speech, declaring the same fundamental truth.

فَٱعْبُدُوهُ - "So worship Him." This is the conclusion of all prophetic messages - pure, undiluted worship of the One God.

هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ - "This is the straight path." There is no ambiguity, no confusion. The path of tawheed is clear and straight.

translation: Then the factions differed [concerning Jesus] from among them, so woe to those who disbelieved - from the scene of a tremendous Day
commentary:
فَٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَيْنِهِمْ ۖ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن مَّشْهَدِ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ

Despite the clarity of Isa's message, فَٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ مِنۢ بَيْنِهِمْ  - "the factions differed among themselves." Notice what's not mentioned - differed about what? About him. About who Isa (AS) really is.

Look at Christianity today - countless denominations, each with their own interpretation of who Jesus is. New denominations continue to emerge, and no two will give you the exact same answer about the nature of Christ. This fragmentation was predicted in this very ayah.

فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن مَّشْهَدِ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ - "Then woe to those who disbelieved from the witnessing of a tremendous Day." The word وَيْلٌ indicates the worst form of destruction. They will witness a great day - the Day of Judgment - and it will bring them ultimate ruin.

translation: How [clearly] they will hear and see the Day they come to Us, but the wrongdoers today are in clear error
commentary:
أَسْمِعْ بِهِمْ وَأَبْصِرْ يَوْمَ يَأْتُونَنَا ۖ لَـٰكِنِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

This ayah contains a powerful irony. أَسْمِعْ بِهِمْ وَأَبْصِرْ - "How well they will hear and how well they will see" - يَوْمَ يَأْتُونَنَا - "on the Day they come to Us."

Think about what you need to follow directions: you need to hear and you need to see. Someone tells you, "Turn right at McDonald's, then left at the light" - you listen to the directions, then you see the landmarks. Both hearing and seeing are necessary to stay on the right path. When you're lost, it means you either didn't hear properly or didn't see properly. You missed something.

But on that Day, these people will hear and see with perfect clarity. The irony is stark: لَـٰكِنِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ ٱلْيَوْمَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ - "But today, the wrongdoers are in clear misguidance." Today, in this world, they refuse to hear and refuse to see. They have the faculties, but they don't use them. On Judgment Day, they'll hear and see everything with perfect clarity, but it will be too late.

translation: And warn them, [O Muhammad], of the Day of Regret, when the matter will be concluded; and [yet], they are in [a state of] heedlessness, and they do not believe
commentary:
وَأَنذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْحَسْرَةِ إِذْ قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ وَهُمْ فِى غَفْلَةٍ وَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

The Prophet ﷺ is commanded: وَأَنذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْحَسْرَةِ - "Warn them of the Day of Regret." الْحَسْرَةِ is deep regret, the kind where you look back and think, "I wish I had done things differently. If only I had another chance."

إِذْ قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ - "When the matter will have been decided." At that point, all decisions are final. There are no appeals, no second chances.

وَهُمْ فِى غَفْلَةٍ - "While they are in heedlessness." Right now, they're completely unaware, living as if that Day will never come.

وَهُمْ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - "And they do not believe." This is the core problem - their disbelief keeps them in heedlessness.

translation: Indeed, it is We who will inherit the earth and whoever is on it, and to Us they will be returned
commentary:
إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَنْ عَلَيْهَا وَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ

This passage about Isa (AS) began with themes of inheritance - remember Zakariya (AS) praying for an heir who would inherit from him and from the family of Ya'qub (AS). Now Allah ﷻ brings us full circle to remind us of the ultimate inheritance.

إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَنْ عَلَيْهَا - "Indeed, We will inherit the earth and whoever is on it." When you inherit something, it means the previous owner left it behind. If you inherit a house from your father, your father is gone and you now own both the house and... your father's place. Allah ﷻ is declaring that He will inherit the earth and everyone on it. The Christians claim "the meek shall inherit the earth," but Allah ﷻ goes further: I will inherit the earth, and all of you.

وَإِلَيْنَا يُرْجَعُونَ - "And to Us they will be returned." Everyone returns to the true Owner.

This passage established tawheed by refuting shirk through the story of Isa (AS). Now that this is clear, we're ready for the next stage of understanding tawheed - through the story of the father of tawheed himself, Ibrahim (AS).

translation: And mention in the Book [the story of] Abraham. Indeed, he was a man of truth and a prophet
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا

The transition is deliberate. First, we removed the false god (Isa is not divine). Now we're ready to understand the true God. You can't learn tawheed properly until you first eliminate shirk - لا إله comes before إلا الله.

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ - "And mention in the Book, Ibrahim." Ibrahim (AS) is mentioned because he is the father of the teachings of tawheed, the one who shattered the idols and stood firm in monotheism.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا - "Indeed, he was a man of truth, a prophet." The word صِدِّيقًا means someone intensely truthful, but it carries deeper meanings. A siddiq is someone who, when given a command from their master, fulfills it immediately with complete sincerity. Every time Allah ﷻ asked Ibrahim (AS) to do something, he did it without hesitation. He didn't just accept the truth - he fulfilled all of its rights. He didn't just have Islam in his heart; he did everything he possibly could for it.

translation: [Mention] when he said to his father, "O my father, why do you worship that which does not hear and does not see and will not benefit you at all
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصِرُ وَلَا يُغْنِى عَنكَ شَيْـًٔا

This passage is one of the most beautiful lessons on how to be good to parents, even when they're in the wrong. We've already had multiple references in this surah about being good to parents: Yahya (AS) was good to his parents, Isa (AS) was good to his mother. Now we get a case study - practical application of how to maintain respect while speaking truth.

إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ - "When he said to his father, 'O my father.'" There's something important to understand about the Arabic here. The word أَبِ (abi) means "father," but يَـٰٓأَبَتِ carries additional emotional weight. In English, we don't have a single phrase that captures this. If you go to your father and say, "Dear Father," it sounds like you're reading a letter. It's unnatural. What's more natural is, "Dad, I love you so much. I really appreciate everything you've done for me. Please, I really want to tell you something."

That entire sentiment - the love, respect, appreciation, and gentle plea for attention - is contained in يَـٰٓأَبَتِ. The "t" at the end is love and respect expressed toward the father.

Now watch how Ibrahim (AS) combines this profound respect with critical truth: لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصِرُ وَلَا يُغْنِى عَنكَ شَيْـًٔا - "Why do you worship that which does not hear, does not see, and does not benefit you at all?"

He frames it as a question, as if seeking to understand. He sounds curious rather than confrontational, even though the question itself is deeply critical. It's the technique of asking questions to teach lessons - appearing confused while actually showing the other person how confused they are. "Father, I love you. Help me understand - why do we worship this thing? Can it hear us? Can it see? You carved out these eyes - can they actually see? How does this benefit us?"

It's positioned between curiosity and criticism, giving his father space to think for himself.

translation: O my father, indeed there has come to me of knowledge that which has not come to you, so follow me; I will guide you to an even path
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّى قَدْ جَآءَنِى مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ مَا لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَٱتَّبِعْنِىٓ أَهْدِكَ صِرَٰطًا سَوِيًّا

The questions weren't enough. His father isn't responding the way he should. Ibrahim (AS) realizes he needs to be more direct, but notice how he starts: يَـٰٓأَبَتِ again. He's already said "Dear Father, I love and respect you," but he says it again. You can never show enough love to your parents. Even when the conversation gets difficult, you keep expressing that love and respect.

إِنِّى قَدْ جَآءَنِى مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ مَا لَمْ يَأْتِكَ - "Indeed, there has come to me of knowledge that which has not come to you." This is remarkably respectful. He's not saying, "I'm smarter than you" or "You're ignorant." He's saying, "Something from knowledge has come particularly to me that hasn't come to you." It's a statement of fact delivered with humility. He acknowledges his father's position as elder while gently asserting that in this matter, he has been given special understanding.

فَٱتَّبِعْنِىٓ أَهْدِكَ صِرَٰطًا سَوِيًّا - "So follow me; I will guide you to a sound path." The word سَوِيًّا means complete, healthy, straight, without gaps or defects. It's a path that's full and whole.

Where's the elder respect? It's in يَـٰٓأَبَتِ, said again and again.

translation: O my father, do not worship Satan. Indeed Satan has ever been, to the Most Merciful, disobedient
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ لَا تَعْبُدِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَصِيًّا

Once more: يَـٰٓأَبَتِ - "O my father." The conversation continues, and you can imagine that between each of Ibrahim's statements, his father is saying harsh things. But Allah ﷻ doesn't dignify those disrespectful responses by recording them. What we're learning here is the persistence of Ibrahim (AS) and how he remained on point. He didn't let the harsh words distract him from what he needed to communicate.

لَا تَعْبُدِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ  - "Do not worship the devil." His father worshiped idols, not the devil directly. But Ibrahim (AS) is giving him a reality check: what you're doing is not the worship of these statues - it's the worship of Shaytan. He's letting his father see the reality behind the action.

إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَصِيًّا - "Indeed, the devil has ever been disobedient to the Most Merciful." The message is clear: the path you're on is the path of the one who rebelled against Rahman.

translation: O my father, indeed I fear that there will touch you a punishment from the Most Merciful so you would be to Satan a companion [in Hellfire]
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يَمَسَّكَ عَذَابٌ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ فَتَكُونَ لِلشَّيْطَـٰنِ وَلِيًّا

For the fourth time: يَـٰٓأَبَتِ. The love and respect never waver, even as the message becomes more urgent.

إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يَمَسَّكَ عَذَابٌ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ - "Indeed, I fear that punishment from the Most Merciful will touch you."

Notice the powerful contrast: عَذَابٌ from ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ. How can the Extremely Merciful punish? Because what his father is doing will overwhelm even that mercy. It will disqualify him from it. Even Rahman's infinite mercy has boundaries - persistent rejection can place you beyond its reach.

فَتَكُونَ لِلشَّيْطَـٰنِ وَلِيًّا - "And you would then be an ally to the devil." The implication is powerful: you will be dealt with just as Shaytan is dealt with. Your job, by manufacturing and selling idols, is the same as Shaytan's job - to misguide others. You've become his partner in this work. You'll be considered right alongside him.

This is a profound lesson for us. When dealing with an elder who is doing wrong - perhaps a parent with a drinking problem, or engaged in any sin - we must find a way to be both respectful and honest. What we usually do is get fired up, and in that fire, respect and love disappear. Only the harsh criticism remains: "What you're doing is shirk! This culture isn't Islam! These practices are stupid!"

Ibrahim (AS) teaches us differently. He maintained two things throughout this conversation: first, respect and love, expressed through يَـٰٓأَبَتِ repeated again and again; and second, unwavering honesty about the truth.

We're not here to change anybody - that's Allah's domain. But we cannot give up on speaking the truth respectfully. Ibrahim (AS) kept trying, and the only time we see him stop is when his father finally says, "Get out of my house. I don't want to hear this anymore."

May Allah ﷻ help us learn from our father Ibrahim (AS) how to be respectful to our parents while remaining honest with our families, speaking truth when the time comes with wisdom and love.

translation: [His father] said, "Have you no desire for my gods, O Abraham? If you do not desist, I will surely stone you, so avoid me a prolonged time
commentary:
قَالَ أَرَاغِبٌ أَنتَ عَنْ ءَالِهَتِى يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ ۖ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ لَأَرْجُمَنَّكَ ۖ وَٱهْجُرْنِى مَلِيًّا

The father's response to Ibrahim (AS) is filled with shock and anger. The phrase أَرَاغِبٌ أَنتَ (are you turning away) uses a reverse sequencing that emphasizes his disbelief. He cannot fathom that his son would abandon the family tradition.

What makes this moment particularly striking is the use of the ism form - رَاغِبٌ - which indicates a permanent state rather than a temporary action. The father has come to a devastating realization: this is not a phase his young son is going through. Ibrahim (AS) is truly committed to leaving the path of his forefathers. He will not be turning back.

The word رَاغِبٌ itself carries deep significance. Later in the Quran, Allah ﷻ will ask: "Who would turn away from the legacy of Ibrahim except one who fools himself?" (2:130). The same root word is used. The irony is that it wasn't Ibrahim (AS) turning away from truth - it was his father turning away from the legacy that was meant to be there all along.

His father's threat - لَأَرْجُمَنَّكَ (I will stone you) - could be taken literally, though it may also be the kind of extreme language parents sometimes use when they're at their breaking point. "I will stone you to death if you don't stop this."

Then comes the final command: وَٱهْجُرْنِى مَلِيًّا - "Get out of my face! Abandon me and migrate far away from me for a long time." The word مَلِيًّا doesn't just mean distance in space, but distance in time. Don't come back anytime soon. The relationship is severed.

translation: [Abraham] said, "Peace will be upon you. I will ask forgiveness for you of my Lord. Indeed, He is ever gracious to me
commentary:
قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكَ ۖ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِى حَفِيًّا

Ibrahim (AS) is now leaving the house. Does he love his father? Absolutely. Is he upset? Yes. But as he walks away, his response is سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكَ - "Peace be upon you." 

Notice the nobility in this word. Salam doesn't just mean peace - it carries within it dignity and honor. Even in this moment of separation, even as he's being cast out, Ibrahim (AS) elevates the conversation. He doesn't respond to harshness with harshness.

سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّىٓ - "I will ask my Master to forgive you." He promises to make istighfar specifically for his father. But then he says something remarkable: إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِى حَفِيًّا - "Indeed, He has always been persistently enquiring about me."

The word حَفِيًّا means someone who asks about someone repeatedly, continuously checking on them. Imagine the scene: the father is kicking his son out of the house. Somewhere inside, despite his anger, the father feels concern. Who will take care of my boy? Where will he sleep? Who will look after him day after day?

Ibrahim (AS) senses this unspoken worry in his father's heart, and he addresses it: "My Master has always been the One who checks on me, who enquires about me constantly. Yes, I'm leaving your care, Dad, but I'm in the care of Allah ﷻ. He will not let too long a time go by before He comes and takes care of me."

translation: And I will leave you and those you invoke other than Allah and will invoke my Lord. I expect that I will not be in invocation to my Lord unhappy
commentary:
وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَمَا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَدْعُوا۟ رَبِّى عَسَىٰٓ أَلَّآ أَكُونَ بِدُعَآءِ رَبِّى شَقِيًّا

He has left the house. Now you can picture him walking through the streets of the city he's about to abandon. Notice the shift: he's no longer speaking directly to his father. The verb changes from second person to plural - وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ (I am abandoning all of you).

The word اعتزال means to separate yourself, to become a loner, to stay away. "I'm going to stay away from all of you and everything you call upon besides Allah ﷻ." This is the same word we saw earlier in the surah with Maryam (AS): فَٱنتَبِذَتْ - she withdrew herself.

وَأَدْعُوا۟ رَبِّى - "And I will call upon my Master." Then something beautiful happens in his language: عَسَىٰٓ أَلَّآ أَكُونَ بِدُعَآءِ رَبِّى شَقِيًّا - "Perhaps I will not be hopeless in the dua I make to my Lord."

Wait - when he spoke about asking forgiveness for his father, he said definitively: سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ - "I will ask forgiveness." But when it comes to his own spiritual state, he doesn't say "I definitely won't be depressed in my dua." He says عَسَىٰٓ - "hopefully, perhaps."

This is the humility of prophets. Ibrahim (AS) doesn't attribute self-righteousness to himself. He's worried about his own future. He doesn't even know for certain if he'll remain on the straight path. His humility is captured in this single word - عَسَىٰٓ.

translation: So when he had left them and those they worshipped other than Allah, We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and each [of them] We made a prophet
commentary:
فَلَمَّا ٱعْتَزَلَهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا نَبِيًّا

Notice how Allah ﷻ repeats the phrase: فَلَمَّا ٱعْتَزَلَهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - "When he abandoned them and what they worshiped besides Allah ﷻ." The repetition emphasizes something critical.

Then came the reward: وَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ - "We granted him Ishaq and Yaqub." These boys needed to be raised in a pure environment. They shouldn't be influenced by a grandfather engaged in shirk. They shouldn't grow up in an idolatrous society.

First, Ibrahim (AS) had to remove himself from that toxic environment. He had to establish: مَا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - abandoning what they call upon besides Allah ﷻ. Only then does Allah ﷻ say: We granted him Ishaq, and then Yaqub - his son and his grandson.

وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا نَبِيًّا - "And each one of them We made a prophet."

translation: And We gave them of Our mercy, and we made for them a reputation of high honor
commentary:
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِنَا وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا

وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِنَا - "And We granted them from Our mercy." But then Allah ﷻ describes a specific quality: وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا - "We gave them a tongue that speaks high truth."

This is what Allah ﷻ had granted Ibrahim (AS) - the ability to speak truth while maintaining the highest standard of speech. We just witnessed this. Look at how Ibrahim (AS) spoke to his father. He held onto truth uncompromisingly, but he never lowered himself to disrespectful language. He spoke with dignity: سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكَ.

This is لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا - a tongue committed to truth (صِدْق) that maintains elevated, noble expression (عَلِيًّا). He didn't resort to low language. He didn't match his father's harshness. 

And now Allah ﷻ tells us: This quality that Ibrahim (AS) had? We gave it to his children too. Even though Allah ﷻ didn't grant him a father on guidance, He granted him children and grandchildren on guidance, and they would carry this same characteristic.

translation: And mention in the Book, Moses. Indeed, he was chosen, and he was a messenger and a prophet
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مُوسَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مُخْلَصًا وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا

Now the narrative shifts: وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مُوسَىٰٓ - "Mention in the Book, Musa."

Notice the sequence of prophets mentioned so far. First came Zakariyya (AS), then through Maryam (AS) we learned of Isa (AS), then Ibrahim (AS), and now Musa (AS). We're not following strict chronology here - there's a deliberate pattern.

What's significant is that Musa (AS) comes from the same blessed lineage we just discussed: from Ishaq (AS) and Yaqub (AS). From that family tree came the Children of Israel, and from them, Musa (AS). We're still within the family of Ibrahim (AS).

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مُخْلَصًا - "Indeed, he was mukhlas" - one made sincere, one chosen. This is a specific term the Quran uses for prophets. While مُخْلِص (with a kasrah) can describe anyone who is sincere in their worship, مُخْلَص (with a fatha) - the one made sincere - is reserved for those divinely chosen and purified.

وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا - "And he was a messenger, a prophet." 

When the Quran mentions both terms together - رَسُول and نَبِىّ - it's highlighting the distinction between them. There's a principle in Arabic: when two things come together, the emphasis is on their differences; when they appear separately, they may carry the same general meaning.

The more convincing scholarly opinion is that a رَسُول (messenger) is sent to a nation with a message to deliver, from the word رِسَالَة (message). A نَبِىّ (prophet) comes from نَبَأ (news) - he delivers news to people but may not be responsible for delivering it to an entire nation, like Yusuf (AS). Or the نَبِىّ may assist the رَسُول, as Harun (AS) assisted Musa (AS).

translation: And We called him from the side of the mount at [his] right and brought him near, confiding [to him]
commentary:
وَنَـٰدَيْنَـٰهُ مِن جَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنِ وَقَرَّبْنَـٰهُ نَجِيًّا

وَنَـٰدَيْنَـٰهُ مِن جَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنِ - "We called to him from the right side of the mountain of Tur."

ٱلْأَيْمَن - the rightmost side, the blessed side.

وَقَرَّبْنَـٰهُ نَجِيًّا - "And We brought him close for private conversation." The word نَجِىّ refers to intimate, confidential discourse. When the brothers of Yusuf (AS) were in complete despair, the Quran says خَلَصُوا۟ نَجِيًّا - they went aside for private consultation.

Allah ﷻ wanted to have an exclusive, private meeting with Musa (AS). This is the honor being described: وَقَرَّبْنَـٰهُ نَجِيًّا - We brought him close to take him into Our confidence.

translation: And We gave him out of Our mercy his brother Aaron as a prophet
commentary:
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥ مِن رَّحْمَتِنَآ أَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ نَبِيًّا

وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥ مِن رَّحْمَتِنَآ أَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ نَبِيًّا - "And We granted him from Our mercy his brother Harun as a prophet."

Prophethood itself is described as a gift - a mercy from Allah ﷻ given to Musa (AS). This is why in traditional Islamic scholarship, Harun (AS) is sometimes called هِبَةُ اللَّهِ - the gift of Allah ﷻ - meaning the gift given to Musa (AS).

translation: And mention in the Book, Ishmael. Indeed, he was true to his promise, and he was a messenger and a prophet
commentary:
وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صَادِقَ ٱلْوَعْدِ وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا

Now we shift to the other side of Ibrahim's family: وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ - "Mention in the Book, Ismail."

We explored one branch through Ishaq (AS), and now we turn to the other branch through Ismail (AS).

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صَادِقَ ٱلْوَعْدِ - "Indeed, he was true to his promise." This isn't just saying he always kept his promises in general. The focus is on a specific, monumental promise: the promise he made to his father to comply with the vision. When Ibrahim (AS) saw in his dream that he was sacrificing his son, and when he told Ismail (AS) about it, his son's response was: "You will find me, if Allah ﷻ wills, among the patient" (37:102).

صَادِقَ ٱلْوَعْدِ - He was true to that promise. He fulfilled it.

translation: And he used to enjoin on his people prayer and zakah and was to his Lord pleasing
commentary:
وَكَانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُۥ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ وَكَانَ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ مَرْضِيًّا

وَكَانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُۥ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ - "And he used to command his family to prayer and zakah."

We've seen this pattern before: وَأْمُرْ أَهْلَكَ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ (20:132). This quality runs through both lines of Ibrahim's family. The Quran is teaching us: it's not just in one branch - it's in the other branch too.

These prophets commanded their families to establish salah and give zakah. This addresses both the Christian audience and the people of Makkah, reminding them: your father Ibrahim (AS) and his descendants were people of salah and zakah.

وَكَانَ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ مَرْضِيًّا - "And he was, with his Master, well-pleased" - or more accurately, one whom his Master was pleased with.

## Ayah 56-57

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِدْرِيسَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا
وَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ مَكَانًا عَلِيًّا

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِدْرِيسَ - "Mention in the Book, Idris."

Idris (AS) precedes all the prophets we've discussed. Some scholars argue he lived a thousand years before Nuh (AS), making him an extremely ancient prophet.

إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا - "Indeed, he was a siddiq, a prophet." Again, the word صِدِّيق is used - one who immediately confirms and upholds the truth.

وَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ مَكَانًا عَلِيًّا - "And We raised him to a high place."

This could be understood figuratively - he enjoyed a very high status as a prophet. The word رَفْع (elevation) often carries metaphorical meanings of honor and rank. Some argue it could also mean he was literally raised to a high place as one of his miracles, but we have no direct text to prove this. Generally, when رَفْع is used and qualified by مَكَانًا (a place), and especially عَلِيًّا (high), it indicates elevated status and rank.

translation: And mention in the Book, Idrees. Indeed, he was a man of truth and a prophet
And We raised him to a high station

Those were the ones upon whom Allah bestowed favor from among the prophets of the descendants of Adam and of those We carried [in the ship] with Noah, and of the descendants of Abraham and Israel, and of those whom We guided and chose. When the verses of the Most Merciful were recited to them, they fell in prostration and weeping
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِن ذُرِّيَّةِ ءَادَمَ وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنَا وَٱجْتَبَيْنَآ ۚ إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ خَرُّوا۟ سُجَّدًا وَبُكِيًّا ۩

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم - "Those are the ones upon whom Allah ﷻ bestowed favor."

مِّنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ - from among the prophets:

مِن ذُرِّيَّةِ ءَادَمَ - from the descendants of Adam (AS) - this refers to Idris (AS).

وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ - and from those We carried with Nuh (AS) - meaning those who descended from the survivors of the flood.

وَمِن ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ - and from the descendants of Ibrahim (AS).

وَإِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ - and Israel, meaning Yaqub (AS) and his descendants.

وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنَا وَٱجْتَبَيْنَآ - and from those We guided and chose.

Despite all this history, despite these different lineages and time periods, Allah ﷻ tells us these prophets had one thing in common:

إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ خَرُّوا۟ سُجَّدًا وَبُكِيًّا - "Whenever the ayat of Ar-Rahman were recited to them, they would fall down in prostration, weeping."

Notice how the name Ar-Rahman is repeated again. When they heard the words of the Excessively Merciful, the response was immediate and visceral: they fell into sajdah, and they couldn't help but cry.

This is the defining characteristic of all these blessed prophets. This is what united them across time, across lineages, across nations.

translation: But there came after them successors who neglected prayer and pursued desires; so they are going to meet evil
commentary:
فَخَلَفَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا

فَخَلَفَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ - "Then after them came successors..."

The word خَلْف is profound here. In Arabic, there are two words: خَلَف (khalaf) and خَلْف (khalf). When you leave behind good children, righteous generations who carry on your legacy, they're called خَلَف. When you leave behind disappointing, corrupt generations, they're called خَلْف.

The difference? Just a vowel. But that small difference in pronunciation captures an enormous difference in meaning. The Arabic language is powerful even in its syllables.

The fatha (the "a" sound) indicates growth and movement - like نَمَا يَنْمُو (growth) or كَبُرَ يَكْبُرُ (to grow, develop). A nation that was constantly moving forward, developing - that's indicated by the fatha.

But when you change it to a damma (the "u" sound), something shifts. The word becomes stagnant. كِبْر (kibr) is arrogance - because the arrogant person is spiritually and intellectually stagnant. He doesn't grow in his worship, doesn't develop as a human being. He's satisfied where he is.

Similarly, خَلَف was an active generation that moved and accomplished things. خَلْف is a generation that sat back and did nothing. The voweling itself illustrates their stagnation.

أَضَاعُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ - "They wasted the prayer."

Allah ﷻ doesn't say they abandoned prayer completely. He says they wasted it. To waste something means it was valuable and you didn't take advantage of it. Every prayer is an opportunity - a treasure. When the time for salah comes, those are precious hours to be seized. These people let them slip away.

Remember what we just read about Ismail (AS)? وَكَانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُۥ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ - "He used to command his family to prayer." That was the legacy. And now this generation wastes it.

وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ - "And they followed their desires."

Again, there's a contrast here. Earlier, the prophets were described with zakah - purification. The alternative to purification is following شَهَوَات - the base, animalistic temptations of human beings.

فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا - "Soon they will meet ghayy."

Someone reading the Quran without appreciating Arabic literature might find this strange. They're already misguided - they've wasted prayer, they've followed desires. So why does Allah ﷻ say they will "meet misguidance soon"? Aren't they already misguided?

This is a beautiful feature of Arabic expression. The Arabs speak about consequences by mentioning the thing that caused them. They say: "The sky rained the greenery." Did the sky rain greenery? No - it rained water, which resulted in greenery. The consequence is mentioned instead of the cause.

Similarly here: they will meet misguidance means they will meet the consequences of misguidance, the results of misguidance.

The word غَىّ itself is powerful. It means to go so far off track that you can't find your way back. You're lost beyond recovery.

This could also be understood literally for the Day of Judgment: they will think they're headed toward Paradise, but then Allah ﷻ will direct them the other way, and they will never be able to find their way back. They will be lost eternally in that misguidance they chose in this world.

translation: Except those who repent, believe and do righteousness; for those will enter Paradise and will not be wronged at all
commentary:
إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا

"Except the ones who repented and revived their faith." This verse presents a powerful sequence that challenges our typical understanding. Usually, we think the person who has Iman will make tawbah. Yet Allah ﷻ mentions tawbah first and Iman second. It is as though you have to come to Iman all over again.

The Prophet ﷺ taught us this reality: "A thief is not a believer while he's stealing. The one drinking alcohol is not a believer at the time they are drinking alcohol." When they make tawbah, it's as if they have to revive their Iman over again. The moment they revive the Iman, now "and he acted righteously" follows.

Notice the shift from plural to singular in this ayah. Previously, the passage spoke of those who "maintain the prayer and give zakah" in the plural form. But here, when it comes to tawbah, the verse shifts to the singular: مَن تَابَ (man taba) - "whoever repents." Why this change? Because the responsibility of tawbah is not a collective one - it's an individual one. People may fall into bad practices because of bad company, but the coming back to the right way cannot be blamed on lack of good company. Even though companionship is important, you remain responsible for your own tawbah. Nobody will make that possible for you.

However, when speaking of entering Jannah, the verse immediately returns to the plural: فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ - "then they will all be entering Jannah." You don't want to be alone in paradise. There's a profound wisdom here captured in the grammatical shifts of the Quran.

The verse concludes with a guarantee: وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا - "and they will not be wronged in any way, shape or form."

translation: [Therein are] gardens of perpetual residence which the Most Merciful has promised His servants in the unseen. Indeed, His promise has ever been coming
commentary:
جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ٱلَّتِى وَعَدَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ عِبَادَهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ وَعْدُهُۥ مَأْتِيًّا

The Gardens of Eden - جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ. Eden is a biblical name as well, and it refers to one of the levels of paradise. It's a proper noun, which explains why the definite article الَّتِى is used in this construction.

These are the gardens "which Ar-Rahman promised His slaves بِٱلْغَيْبِ - in the unseen." Allah ﷻ kept describing them throughout the Quran - gardens and rivers and trees and fruits - but none of it has been seen by us. As the Prophet ﷺ said, what Allah ﷻ has prepared is "what no eye has ever seen."

The verse concludes with absolute certainty: إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ وَعْدُهُۥ مَأْتِيًّا. The word مَأْتِيًّا carries the meaning of something that will inevitably be brought about. This is a promise that Allah ﷻ Himself will bring to reality - there is no doubt in it whatsoever.

translation: They will not hear therein any ill speech - only [greetings of] peace - and they will have their provision therein, morning and afternoon
commentary:
لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا إِلَّا سَلَـٰمًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيهَا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا

They will not hear any لَغْوًا - useless, empty, vain, insulting, hurtful or pointless talk. Their conversations in paradise will be meaningful and enjoyable. Some might wonder: if there is no laghw, how will paradise be paradise? If we can't just freely speak our minds, where's the enjoyment? Allah ﷻ removes speech that leads to evil. Sometimes speech in itself can be harmful, but even when it seems merely useless, what it leads to can be far worse.

The phrase إِلَّا سَلَـٰمًا  - "except peace" - requires careful understanding. This is what Arabic grammarians call الاستثناء المنقطع, a disconnected exception. The meaning is: "They won't hear bad speech - unless you consider peace to be bad speech." It's like saying in English: "That person has no flaws, unless you consider bravery a flaw." The Arabs would emphasize praise by making it comparable to what's being criticized. So if you think peace is somehow objectionable, well, they're going to get a lot of that in paradise.

وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيهَا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا - "And they will have their provisions in it, morning and evening." The word رِزْقُهُمْ - "their provisions" - is striking. It suggests something they themselves earned. They're going to be compensated for the deeds they did with specific meals. This delicacy is a result of that prayer; that provision comes from this act of charity.

translation: That is Paradise, which We give as inheritance to those of Our servants who were fearing of Allah
commentary:
تِلْكَ ٱلْجَنَّةُ ٱلَّتِى نُورِثُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا مَن كَانَ تَقِيًّا

تِلْكَ ٱلْجَنَّةُ - "That is the garden." That Jannah, that lush garden, is the one that We are going to give as an inheritance to some of Our slaves. From all of mankind, من كَانَ تَقِيًّا- whoever had been protective and watchful, whoever had taqwa.

translation: [Gabriel said], "And we [angels] descend not except by the order of your Lord. To Him belongs that before us and that behind us and what is in between. And never is your Lord forgetful
commentary:
وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ ۖ لَهُۥ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا

This ayah marks an interesting shift in the narrative. In the context of revelation, receiving the Quran was very heavy and difficult for the Prophet ﷺ. As Allah ﷻ says, إِنَّا سَنُلْقِى عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا - "Indeed, We will cast upon you a heavy word" (73:5). Yet despite the difficulty of receiving it, the joy and blessing afterward made the Prophet ﷺ desire more and more of it. 

One time, the Prophet ﷺ told Jibreel (AS): "You should come more often." In response, this ayah was revealed, with Jibreel (AS) being the speaker: وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ - "We don't come down except when your Master commands."

Notice the profound adab of Jibreel (AS). He doesn't speak about himself by saying "I" or "me." Instead, he uses the plural "we" and then says: لَهُۥ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ - "To Him belongs whatever is in front of us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between that."
