When he says "what is in between," he's referring to himself and those with him, but he doesn't have the audacity to say "and us." Rather, he says "whatever is in between that" - whatever is left between what's in front and what's behind, which includes himself.

Why does Jibreel (AS) use "we"? Because he is مُكَرَّمُونَ - one of the honored angels - and he doesn't come alone. He comes with a legion of angels to protect the revelation. Jibreel (AS) himself delivers the revelation, but there's an entire battalion that shows up and stands guard while the Prophet ﷺ is receiving his revelation. So he speaks on behalf of the entire group.

The ayah concludes with reassurance: وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا - "And your Master doesn't forget." Allah ﷻ brings revelation on time. He hasn't forgotten about you, and He never loses track of you.

translation: Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them - so worship Him and have patience for His worship. Do you know of any similarity to Him
commentary:
رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فَٱعْبُدْهُ وَٱصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَـٰدَتِهِۦ ۚ هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُۥ سَمِيًّا

رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا - "The Master of the skies and the earth and whatever is in between them." Despite managing all of this - despite how many things He has to take care of - He doesn't forget. Human beings, when busy with many things, become distracted. But not Allah ﷻ.

فَٱعْبُدْهُ - "So continue to worship Him." Whether revelation comes or not, focus on worship. وَٱصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَـٰدَتِهِۦ - "And be patient with yourself in maintaining yourself in worship of Him."

The ayah concludes with a rhetorical question: هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُۥ سَمِيًّا - "Have you ever heard anyone with the name that He has? Have you ever known of anybody that has the qualities that Allah ﷻ has?"

This is beautiful praise of Allah ﷻ. Among all the shirk committed by the mushrikun, they never named a false god "Allah." The word Allah was never shared by a false god - this is one of the miracles of language. And even if someone were named Allah, have you ever known of anyone that has the qualities that He has? The word سَمِيًّا refers not just to the name itself, but to the qualities that name represents. That's why we say الأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَىٰ - He has the most beautiful names, because He has the most beautiful qualities.

translation: And the disbeliever says, "When I have died, am I going to be brought forth alive
commentary:
وَيَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَءِذَا مَا مِتُّ لَسَوْفَ أُخْرَجُ حَيًّا

Allah ﷻ just said وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا - "Your Master doesn't forget." Now notice the contrast: وَيَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ - "And the human being says." The word إِنسَـٰنُ comes from the same root as نَسِيَ - to forget. Allah ﷻ doesn't forget, but the human being - whose very name suggests forgetfulness - says:

أَءِذَا مَا مِتُّ لَسَوْفَ أُخْرَجُ حَيًّا - "When I die - whenever I die, one day eventually - am I really going to be raised again as a living being?" The phrasing أَءِذَا مَا مِتُّ (a-idha ma mittu) emphasizes distance and doubt: "way out there when I die."

translation: Does man not remember that We created him before, while he was nothing
commentary:
أَوَلَا يَذْكُرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ يَكُ شَيْـًٔا

أَوَلَا يَذْكُرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ - "Does the human being not remember and acknowledge?" أَنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ يَكُ شَيْـًٔا - "That We created him way before, and he was nothing to begin with?"

If Allah ﷻ created you from nothing the first time, is it really so difficult to believe He can recreate you?

translation: So by your Lord, We will surely gather them and the devils; then We will bring them to be present around Hell upon their knees
commentary:
فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ وَٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ ثُمَّ لَنُحْضِرَنَّهُمْ حَوْلَ جَهَنَّمَ جِثِيًّا

فَوَرَبِّكَ- "Then I swear by your Master." This is one of the few instances in the Quran where Allah ﷻ swears by Himself. لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ وَٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ - "We will raise them, gather them, and the devils along with them." The devils that gave them false promises, that whispered to them - they will all be raised together.

ثُمَّ لَنُحْضِرَنَّهُمْ حَوْلَ جَهَنَّمَ جِثِيًّا - "Then We will bring them all around Jahannam." Imagine Jahannam as this round place, and they're sitting all around it. جِثِيًّا means sitting on one's knees, in the position of someone about to be executed. People that are supposed to be punished are forced onto their knees. Allah ﷻ says they will be brought right in front of Jahannam, seated on their knees.

translation: Then We will surely extract from every sect those of them who were worst against the Most Merciful in insolence
commentary:
ثُمَّ لَنَنزِعَنَّ مِن كُلِّ شِيعَةٍ أَيُّهُمْ أَشَدُّ عَلَى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عِتِيًّا

ثُمَّ لَنَنزِعَنَّ - "Then We will pull out, We will yank out." The word نَزَعَ means to pull something forcefully from its origin, from the inside. مِن كُلِّ شِيعَةٍ - "From every single faction" - every religious faction, every ideological group.

أَيُّهُمْ أَشَدُّ عَلَى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عِتِيًّا - "Who of them was the biggest bully against Ar-Rahman? Who was the one that stood up to Ar-Rahman and made fun of Him?" Allah ﷻ will pull the biggest criminals out of each group and start yanking them away. The use of نَزَعَ implies they will want to stay with their people - "No, no, no!" - but they're being dragged one by one. The big leaders, the ringleaders, are extracted first.

The word عِتِيًّا comes from عَتَا, which refers to someone who bullies and overpowers others. It also carries the meaning of old age - that relentless force that no one can stand up to. These were people who thought themselves so powerful, who believed they could stand up to anyone. Now they're being yanked out.

translation: Then, surely it is We who are most knowing of those most worthy of burning therein
commentary:
ثُمَّ لَنَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِهَا صِلِيًّا

ثُمَّ لَنَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِهَا صِلِيًّا - "Then We are truly the ones that know better who gets priority in getting thrown into it." 

صِلِيًّا comes from وَصَلَ, meaning to be cast into the fire. The big criminals are lined up now - all the all-star criminals - and Allah ﷻ says: "Let Me pick who goes in first. Who goes first?" This psychological torture is being described in the Quran. These are very frightening ayahs.

translation: And there is none of you except he will come to it. This is upon your Lord an inevitability decreed
commentary:
وَإِن مِّنكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا ۚ كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَّقْضِيًّا

وَإِن مِّنكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا - "There is not a single one among you except that they will pass by it." The word وَارِدُهَا can mean to pass by something, but it can also mean to enter. Some scholars, based on narrations found in certain books of hadith, say that Muslims will actually go inside the Hellfire but will be protected from its flames - just as Ibrahim (AS) was protected from the fire when he was thrown into it. According to this view, we will take an inside tour of Jahannam before entering Jannah.

Other scholars say we will pass over it or by it, witnessing its horrors without entering.

And just in case you think you might be the exception, Allah ﷻ emphasizes: كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَّقْضِيًّا - "That has been declared. The verdict has been finalized." 

حَتْمًا means absolutely final. In Arabic, when they wanted to emphasize something was definitively known, they would say حَتْمًا - this is certain beyond doubt. This verdict - that everyone will pass by Hell, even those going to paradise - will get to see it and witness it before they enter Jannah. They need to know what they've escaped, what they've been saved from. They will have to see it. It's a final verdict; there's no escape from it.

translation: Then We will save those who feared Allah and leave the wrongdoers within it, on their knees
commentary:
ثُمَّ نُنَجِّى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّنَذَرُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا

ثُمَّ نُنَجِّى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ - "Then, after some time" - notice ثُمَّ indicates a delay, not immediate action - "We will rescue those who truly protected themselves."

وَّنَذَرُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا  - "And We will abandon the wrongdoers in it, on their knees once again." Just as they first came on their knees, seated around Jahannam, they are pushed into the fire while still on their knees. May Allah ﷻ protect us from being among them.

translation: And when Our verses are recited to them as clear evidences, those who disbelieve say to those who believe, "Which of [our] two parties is best in position and best in association
commentary:
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَّقَامًا وَأَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا

Now Allah ﷻ describes how these people earned their place in Jahannam. وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ - "When Our miraculous signs are recited to them in the most clear way" - when these signs that are so clear and obvious are presented to them - قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ - "the disbelievers say to the believers something outrageous":

أَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَّقَامًا - "Which of the two groups stands in a better position?" Look at yourselves, Muslims. Look at your homes and look at our homes. Look at your military and look at our military. Who has the leadership and who doesn't? Who's starving and who has all the resources? Who controls the Ka'bah and who doesn't? Who has a pathetic following of slaves and youth, and who has strong men around them?

وَأَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا - "And better in council?" The word نَدِيًّا refers to the meeting place of high-level executives and officials. The Quraysh would say: "Compare yourselves to us. You have your meeting and you sit around Bilal (RA) and Suhayb Ar-Rumi (RA) and Salman Al-Farsi (RA). This is your high-level meeting, and you're comparing yourself to us? Our high-level meetings include the leaders of Quraysh, the elders, the millionaires. Our meetings are held in castles, in the high courts. Your meetings are held outdoors by the Ka'bah. You're going to compare yourselves to us?"

They had this arrogant attitude about where they stood in society. This is the mindset Allah ﷻ is describing.

translation: And how many a generation have We destroyed before them who were better in possessions and [outward] appearance
commentary:
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَحْسَنُ أَثَـٰثًا وَرِءْيًا

Allah's response: وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ - "How many nations before them have We destroyed?" هُمْ أَحْسَنُ أَثَـٰثًا - "They were far superior in terms of furnishings, provisions and utilities."

You think you have resources? You barely have a well of water in Makkah, and that exists only by the miracle of Allah ﷻ. What else do you have? You have no resources, no utilities, no advanced technologies. We destroyed nations that were far more advanced than you.

وَرِءْيًا - "And they were far better in terms of appearance and show." Look at Fir'awn - his ruins are still a tourist attraction. The monuments of past nations still stand as testimony to their former glory. Yet where are those nations now?

translation: Say, "Whoever is in error - let the Most Merciful extend for him an extension [in wealth and time] until, when they see that which they were promised - either punishment [in this world] or the Hour [of resurrection] - they will come to know who is worst in position and weaker in soldiers
commentary:
قُلْ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مَدًّا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَأَوْا۟ مَا يُوعَدُونَ إِمَّا ٱلْعَذَابَ وَإِمَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضْعَفُ جُندًا

قُلْ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مَدًّا - "Tell them: whoever has been lost in misguidance, then Ar-Rahman - the Extremely Merciful - will extend their means."

This extension serves two purposes. If someone wants to remain in misguidance, let them dig their hole deeper. But if they want to get their act together, they have an extension - an opportunity to turn back.

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَأَوْا۟ مَا يُوعَدُونَ - "Until they get to see whatever they have been promised" - إِمَّا ٱلْعَذَابَ - "whether they see the punishment" - meaning even worldly punishment before death - وَإِمَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ - "or they get to see the Day of Judgment itself."

فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا - "Then they will know who stands in the worst place." Remember how they asked أَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَّقَامًا - "which group has the better position?" Now Allah ﷻ says: then they will know who truly has the worst position.

وَأَضْعَفُ جُندًا - "And who has the weaker army." They will find out on that Day.

---

From this point until the end of the surah, the warnings intensify. This is a surah of mercy - it began with Zakariyya (AS) and the good news of a righteous child, and it ends with stark warnings. It opened with good news and concludes with admonition. This mirrors the methodology of the Prophet ﷺ himself. He began delivering his message as good news, and when that fell on deaf ears, the message turned to warning. The surah follows this same pattern - we begin with mercy and conclude with serious warnings about the consequences of rejecting that mercy.

translation: And Allah increases those who were guided, in guidance, and the enduring good deeds are better to your Lord for reward and better for recourse
commentary:
وَيَزِيدُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱهْتَدَوْا۟ هُدًى ۗ وَٱلْبَـٰقِيَـٰتُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ خَيْرٌ عِندَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ مَّرَدًّا

This passage marks the beginning of the final section of Surah Maryam, where Allah ﷻ establishes a clear contrast between the believers and the disbelievers. The dominant theme throughout these closing ayahs will be warning.

Allah ﷻ tells us that He will increase those who have committed themselves to guidance with even more guidance. This is a fundamental principle in the Quran: when someone takes a step toward Allah ﷻ, He grants them more. The path of guidance expands for those who choose to walk it.

The phrase الْبَـٰقِيَـٰتُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ refers to "the remaining good deeds" or "the lasting good works." Whatever small accomplishments believers can achieve in this life become tremendous assets for them in the Hereafter. These deeds are far more valuable than the status and position that Quraysh boasted about. Their worldly power means nothing compared to what these lasting good deeds will be for the believers.

This ayah also echoes a theme from Surah Al-Kahf, where Allah ﷻ says, "But the lasting good deeds are better with your Lord for reward and better for hope" (18:46). Here, Allah ﷻ uses the word مَرَدًّا, which comes from the root meaning "to return something." The imagery is beautiful: every good deed you send forward to Allah ﷻ is like a boomerang. Rewards are added to it, and it comes back to you multiplied. In the previous surah, Allah ﷻ mentioned, "They will find whatever they did present before them" (18:49). Here, He emphasizes that these deeds will return to them with increase.

translation: Then, have you seen he who disbelieved in Our verses and said, "I will surely be given wealth and children [in the next life]
commentary:
أَفَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَقَالَ لَأُوتَيَنَّ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا

Allah ﷻ now addresses a specific manifestation of disbelief that was also discussed in Surah Al-Kahf: the story of the two gardens. The disbeliever in that narrative declared confidently, "I am being given wealth, and I will be given children." His reasoning was simple but flawed: "If God has taken such good care of me in this world, why wouldn't I receive even better in the Hereafter?"

This ayah summarizes that arrogant attitude. The person who rejects Allah's signs believes his material success is guaranteed to continue, both in this life and beyond.

translation: Has he looked into the unseen, or has he taken from the Most Merciful a promise
commentary:
أَطَّلَعَ ٱلْغَيْبَ أَمِ ٱتَّخَذَ عِندَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَهْدًا

Allah ﷻ responds with penetrating questions. Notice the unique form: أَطَّلَعَ instead of the expected اطَّلَعَ. The extra emphasis in the pronunciation reflects the absurdity of the claim.

The word اطَّلَعَ means to obtain news after making effort, particularly by climbing to discover something from a height. Allah ﷻ asks: Did this person climb somewhere to get news of the unseen? Did he ascend to some elevated position where he could peer into the future and confirm his assumptions about what awaits him?

Or does he have some special promise, some exclusive covenant with Ar-Rahman that guarantees him this treatment? The use of Ar-Rahman is deliberate—it emphasizes Allah's universal mercy, not some private arrangement with an individual who rejects His signs.

translation: No! We will record what he says and extend for him from the punishment extensively
commentary:
كَلَّا ۚ سَنَكْتُبُ مَا يَقُولُ وَنَمُدُّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ مَدًّا

كَلَّا —not at all! This emphatic negation dismisses both possibilities. He has neither climbed to knowledge of the unseen nor secured any special promise.

Allah ﷻ declares: We will record what he says. Every obnoxious comment, every arrogant claim, every word of disbelief—all of it is being written down. When they say insulting things like "the believers are bankrupt and we're the ones who are rich," Allah ﷻ records it all. Nothing goes unnoticed.

Then comes the ominous consequence: وَنَمُدُّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ مَدًّا. Every time this person might think the duration of punishment is complete, another extension is added, and then another. In worldly justice systems, we sometimes hear of multiple life sentences—someone sentenced to 500 years in prison. It seems meaningless since a person will die long before that. But in Jahannam, these extensions are real. Multiple life sentences actually exist there, in a state that is neither true life nor true death, but something horrific in between.

translation: And We will inherit him [in] what he mentions, and he will come to Us alone
commentary:
وَنَرِثُهُۥ مَا يَقُولُ وَيَأْتِينَا فَرْدًا

The word نَرِث is profound here. He boasts about wealth and children, about assets and legacy. But Allah ﷻ says: We will inherit all of it. Everything he talks about owning will be stripped away. He will come to Us فَرْدًا —alone, solitary, with none of his wealth, none of his manpower, none of his tribe, nobody to support him.

translation: And they have taken besides Allah [false] deities that they would be for them [a source of] honor
commentary:
وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً لِّيَكُونُوا۟ لَهُمْ عِزًّا

Allah ﷻ now explores another dimension of shirk: the psychological need behind it. They chose gods besides Allah ﷻ, thinking these deities would be a source of عِزّ for them. The word عِزّ carries the meaning of being undefeatable, untouchable. They believed these false gods would shield them from the troubles ahead, whether in this life or the next. This is why people call upon saints, or Jesus (AS), or angels—seeking protection they believe Allah ﷻ alone cannot or will not provide.

translation: No! Those "gods" will deny their worship of them and will be against them opponents [on the Day of Judgement]
commentary:
كَلَّا ۚ سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ وَيَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا

كَلَّا—not at all! The bitter irony is this: those very beings they worshiped will deny their worship. On the Day of Judgment, when these people turn to Jesus (AS) or the angels or their so-called intercessors, those beings will say, "You never worshiped me. What are you talking about? I never asked to be worshiped." They will flat-out deny it to their faces.

And it gets worse. وَيَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا—they will become opponents against them. The word ضِدّ (didd) means the complete opposite, the staunchest adversary. The ones these mushrikeen hoped would testify on their behalf will instead become the prosecution witnesses against them. They will be the most vicious opponents in their case.

translation: Do you not see that We have sent the devils upon the disbelievers, inciting them to [evil] with [constant] incitement
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ تَؤُزُّهُمْ أَزًّا

Allah ﷻ reveals something unsettling: Didn't you see that We sent the devils against the disbelievers? When someone chooses a path of evil, Allah ﷻ allows the shayateen to have their way with them. This is part of the divine principle of extension we saw earlier. Once you pick the wrong path, Allah ﷻ opens it further.

The word تَؤُزُّهُمْ أَزًّا is particularly powerful. أَزّ means to slowly boil, like making stew on low heat. The shayateen don't attack suddenly; they work gradually, heating up the situation degree by degree. It's like the experiment where a frog placed in gradually heating water doesn't jump out and eventually dies—whereas if you drop it in hot water, it immediately leaps to safety.

This is how the shayateen work. They slowly incite feelings, slowly excite people about their false causes. The victims don't realize they're being manipulated or that there's a fire beneath them. By the time they recognize what's happening, it's too late.

translation: So be not impatient over them. We only count out to them a [limited] number
commentary:
فَلَا تَعْجَلْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ إِنَّمَا نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا

Allah ﷻ comforts the Messenger ﷺ: Don't rush regarding their case. Don't be hasty in judging what their verdict should be. Don't be impatient thinking, "Why aren't they being guided? Why doesn't Allah ﷻ just destroy them now?"

No—We are keeping full count of them. We know exactly who they are. إِنَّمَا نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا—We are counting everything they do. Let them have their long lives. Let them build piles upon piles of sin. Don't worry; We have it all recorded.

translation: On the Day We will gather the righteous to the Most Merciful as a delegation
commentary:
يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَفْدًا

This ayah presents a beautiful image of the believers on Judgment Day. The word نَحْشُرُ means to gather, usually with an external force like a shepherd herding animals. There are armies of angels escorting the believers, but notice the state in which they arrive: وَفْدًا.

وَفْد is the plural of وَافِد, and it carries a specific, dignified meaning. A wafd is a delegation that goes to a king who has promised them something. They come to collect what has been set aside for them—not as beggars, but as honored guests with an appointment. In the old days, when people traveled far to meet a king and receive a promised gift, that group was called a wafd.

This is how the people of taqwa will be brought before Ar-Rahman. The word مُتَّقِين takes on a new dimension here. A muttaqi is not only someone who protects himself through consciousness of Allah ﷻ, but also someone who is protected. They are shielded from the horrors of Judgment Day and rushed toward Allah ﷻ. When brought before Him as honored guests, they present the promise He made to them, and He fulfills it generously.

translation: And will drive the criminals to Hell in thirst
commentary:
وَنَسُوقُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ وِرْدًا

In stark contrast, the criminals will be driven. The word نَسُوقُ comes from سَوْق, which means to herd animals. While the believers are escorted with dignity, the criminals are driven like cattle toward Jahannam.

They arrive وِرْدًا (wirdan). وِرْد refers to a well where one goes to draw water, a place someone seeks to quench their thirst. The bitter irony: the place where they will seek to satisfy their thirst will be Jahannam itself.

translation: None will have [power of] intercession except he who had taken from the Most Merciful a covenant
commentary:
لَّا يَمْلِكُونَ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةَ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ عِندَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَهْدًا

They have no authority to secure intercession for themselves. All their beliefs about angels doing shafa'ah, about their ancestors interceding, about saints helping them—completely shattered. This addresses particularly the Christian community's belief in intercession through Jesus (AS).

The only exception: إِلَّا مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ عِندَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَهْدًا (except the one who has taken a covenant with Ar-Rahman). What is this covenant? It's the covenant of tawheed, of true faith and righteous action. It's the promise Allah ﷻ makes with those who believe in Him alone.

translation: And they say, "The Most Merciful has taken [for Himself] a son
commentary:
وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَلَدًا

After teaching the lesson through the story of Zakariya (AS), building up to 'Isa (AS), then discussing Ibrahim (AS) and how their shirk is no different from Ibrahim's father and people, Allah ﷻ now speaks directly and explicitly.

They said: Ar-Rahman has taken a son.

The use of Ar-Rahman here is crucial. The Christians connect God's mercy with His supposed son. They say it is because God is merciful that He had a son. It is God's mercy that allowed this son to sacrifice himself so all sins could be forgiven. They've built an entire theology around manipulating the concept of Allah's mercy, making it dependent on this false narrative of divine sonship.

So Allah ﷻ takes that very name they invoke—Ar-Rahman, the Most Merciful—and shows His anger at this grotesque distortion.

translation: You have done an atrocious thing
commentary:
لَّقَدْ جِئْتُمْ شَيْـًٔا إِدًّا

You have uttered something إِدًّا. This is a new and powerful word. إِدّ refers to a chaotic, shocking sound—like a scream or explosion that you didn't expect, something that creates disorder everywhere. It can also mean something unrecognizable in its evil, outrageous in its offense.

Allah ﷻ is saying: You have come forward with a monstrosity. You have said something horribly disgusting, shockingly offensive.

translation: The heavens almost rupture therefrom and the earth splits open and the mountains collapse in devastation
commentary:
تَكَادُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْهُ وَتَنشَقُّ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَتَخِرُّ ٱلْجِبَالُ هَدًّا

The skies are almost tearing apart because of it. The earth is nearly splitting open. The mountains are on the verge of crumbling down.

Notice the difference between يَتَفَطَّرْنَ (yatafattarna) and تَنشَقُّ (tanshaqqu). فَطْر refers to something long being ripped across its length—the vast expanse of the universe cracking along its span. شَقّ means something hard cracking open. Allah ﷻ says the skies themselves cannot bear this statement. They're about to tear across the entire universe every time someone says Allah ﷻ has taken a son.

It's not just Muslims who are offended by this claim. The entire creation shakes. The universe cannot handle it and is about to explode. These small sound waves coming from a church, from someone in their car saying "Lord Jesus" as the son of God—those waves travel across the span of the universe. All of creation hears them, and the heavens convulse. They cannot tolerate such an offensive sound.

The only thing keeping the universe intact is the word تَكَادُ—"almost." Otherwise, it would be finished. But how does it not crack? How does it reach that breaking point but not shatter? Another ayah tells us: "Indeed, Allah ﷻ holds the heavens and the earth, lest they cease" (35:41). Allah ﷻ Himself is holding the skies and earth together. If it weren't for Him, these words alone would be offensive enough to tear creation apart.

translation: That they attribute to the Most Merciful a son
commentary:
أَن دَعَوْا۟ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَلَدًا

All of this—the near-tearing of the skies, the almost-splitting of the earth, the mountains about to collapse—because they attributed to Ar-Rahman a son.

translation: And it is not appropriate for the Most Merciful that He should take a son
commentary:
وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ أَن يَتَّخِذَ وَلَدًا

It is not appropriate for Ar-Rahman. It is not becoming, not befitting of the Most Merciful that He would take a son. The concept itself contradicts His nature, His majesty, His absolute independence.

translation: There is no one in the heavens and earth but that he comes to the Most Merciful as a servant
commentary:
إِن كُلُّ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّآ ءَاتِى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَبْدًا

There is no one—absolutely no one—in the heavens or the earth except that they come to Ar-Rahman as a slave, as عَبْد. Every living being, without exception, presents itself before Allah ﷻ in the position of servitude. This includes 'Isa (AS), the angels, and every prophet. All are slaves of Allah ﷻ.

The إِلَّا here is حَصْر (exclusivity): the only possible relationship any created being can have with Ar-Rahman is that of slave to Master.

translation: He has enumerated them and counted them a [full] counting
commentary:
لَّقَدْ أَحْصَىٰهُمْ وَعَدَّهُمْ عَدًّا

Don't worry—We have full account of them. أَحْصَىٰهُمْ means We've counted them, and عَدَّهُمْ عَدًّا means We've numbered them precisely. 

The difference is significant: إِحْصَاء is the comprehensive, macro-level record—the complete account. عَدّ is the detailed, micro-level counting—every single instance. Allah ﷻ has both the full record of every person and the detailed account of every action, every word of shirk, every time they utter the offensive claim. Macro and micro, nothing escapes His knowledge.

translation: And all of them are coming to Him on the Day of Resurrection alone
commentary:
وَكُلُّهُمْ ءَاتِيهِ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَرْدًا

Each and every one of them will come to Him on the Day of Resurrection فَرْدًا—alone, utterly alone. No wealth, no family, no tribe, no false gods to help. Every soul faces Allah ﷻ individually, accountable for what they chose to believe and how they chose to live.

translation: Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds - the Most Merciful will appoint for them affection
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وُدًّا

This is a beautifully placed ayah about love, coming right after the false theology that manipulates the concept of divine love. They say "God is love" and build elaborate doctrines around it. Allah ﷻ corrects this and puts love in its proper place.

Those who truly believed and did righteous deeds—Allah ﷻ will place وُدّ in them. وُدّ is a profound, burning form of love. This works in multiple dimensions:

First, Ar-Rahman will have love for them, and they will have love for Allah ﷻ—a mutual, deep affection.

Second, Allah ﷻ will place love in the hearts of believers for one another. You'll see another Muslim and feel joy in your heart. You'll be happy just because there's another person saying لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا ٱللَّٰهُ. Even if you've never met, you recognize each other as Muslim and feel an immediate connection, an unexplainable happiness.

This is the true manifestation of divine love—not through false theology, but through faith and righteous action. This is how Allah's mercy operates, not through the distortion of claiming He needs a son.

translation: So, [O Muhammad], We have only made Qur'an easy in the Arabic language that you may give good tidings thereby to the righteous and warn thereby a hostile people
commentary:
فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَـٰهُ بِلِسَانِكَ لِتُبَشِّرَ بِهِ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ وَتُنذِرَ بِهِۦ قَوْمًا لُّدًّا

Allah ﷻ addresses the Messenger ﷺ directly. We have made this Quran easy for you to deliver on your tongue. Even though the Messenger ﷺ is the most eloquent of all people, Allah ﷻ emphasizes that He has given him the easiest speech to deliver for this purpose.

Why? لِتُبَشِّرَ بِهِ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ—so you can give glad tidings to the people of taqwa. The best way to encourage Muslims, to uplift their hearts, is through the Quran itself.

وَتُنذِرَ بِهِۦ قَوْمًا لُّدًّا—and so you can warn by means of it a people who are ludd. لُدّ describes someone extremely aggressive, intensely argumentative, full of hatred in their opposition. When you try to speak to people about their shirk using the Quran, they will respond with extreme hostility. They will be offensive and combative.

But Allah ﷻ reassures the Prophet ﷺ: This Quran will be enough. We've made this Quran easy for you to deliver this message without any hesitation, even to the most hostile audiences.

translation: And how many have We destroyed before them of generations? Do you perceive of them anyone or hear from them a sound
commentary:
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هَلْ تُحِسُّ مِنْهُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَوْ تَسْمَعُ لَهُمْ رِكْزًۢا

How many nations before them have We destroyed? Look at how the surah ends. Do you even sense that any one of them still exists? Can you feel even a remnant of their existence?

أَوْ تَسْمَعُ لَهُمْ رِكْزًۢا—or do you hear from them a rikz? رِكْز is a new word, referring to the slightest possible sound, like the buzzing of a fly. Can you hear even that much from them?

This is profound. The surah began with a low sound—Zakariya (AS) calling upon his Lord in a whisper, نِدَاءً خَفِيًّا, such a quiet voice full of hope and humility. That was a low sound of good news.

Now Allah ﷻ mentions the lowest of sounds as a warning: I will destroy them so completely that they won't even have the buzzing of an insect left. You will pass by those towns and not even insects will survive there. Nothing will remain—no sound, no trace, no memory.

This is the warning with which this powerful surah concludes. From the gentle whisper of a prophet seeking mercy, to the absolute silence of those who rejected that mercy. The contrast is complete.
